topics_flat: Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Greatest common divisors (gcd)
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Prime numbers
prompt:
$N$ positive integer numbers are given, such that greatest common divisors of all nonempty sets of these numbers are pairwise distinct. Determine the smallest possible number of distinct prime divisors of the product of these $N$ numbers.
(OleksandrGolovanov)
response_text:
Let the numbers be \(a_1,\dots,a_N\), and for every nonempty subset \(S\subseteq \{1,\dots,N\}\), let
\[
g(S)=\gcd(a_i:\ i\in S).
\]
We are told that all these \(2^N-1\) values are pairwise distinct.

We want the minimum possible number of distinct primes dividing
\[
a_1a_2\cdots a_N.
\]

---

## 1. A lower bound

Let \(p_1,\dots,p_k\) be the distinct primes appearing in the product, so each \(a_i\) has a factorization
\[
a_i=\prod_{j=1}^k p_j^{e_{ij}}.
\]
Then for any nonempty subset \(S\),
\[
g(S)=\prod_{j=1}^k p_j^{\min_{i\in S} e_{ij}}.
\]
So \(g(S)\) is determined by the \(k\)-tuple
\[
\bigl(\min_{i\in S} e_{i1},\dots,\min_{i\in S} e_{ik}\bigr).
\]

Now fix one prime \(p_j\). As \(S\) varies, the value \(\min_{i\in S} e_{ij}\) must be one of the \(N\) numbers \(e_{1j},\dots,e_{Nj}\). Thus it has at most \(N\) possible values. Therefore the total number of possible gcds is at most \(N^k\).

But we need at least \(2^N-1\) distinct gcds. Hence
\[
N^k\ge 2^N-1.
\]
So necessarily
\[
k\ge \left\lceil \log_N(2^N-1)\right\rceil.
\]

This gives the lower bound.

---

## 2. Construction attaining the bound

Let
\[
k=\left\lceil \log_N(2^N-1)\right\rceil,
\]
so \(N^k\ge 2^N-1\).

We will encode the \(2^N-1\) nonempty subsets injectively by \(k\)-tuples with entries in \(\{1,2,\dots,N\}\).

For each nonempty subset \(S\), choose a distinct code
\[
c(S)=(c_1(S),\dots,c_k(S))\in \{1,\dots,N\}^k.
\]

Now choose \(k\) distinct primes \(p_1,\dots,p_k\). For each coordinate \(j\), relabel the elements \(1,\dots,N\) by a permutation so that larger subsets correspond to smaller labels if needed; concretely, we only need the following standard representation:

For each \(j\), define a chain of sets
\[
T_{j,1}\supseteq T_{j,2}\supseteq \cdots \supseteq T_{j,N}
\]
by letting \(T_{j,t}\) be the union of all subsets \(S\) with \(c_j(S)\ge t\). Then define exponents
\[
e_{ij}=\max\{t:\ i\in T_{j,t}\}.
\]
This ensures that for each nonempty \(S\),
\[
\min_{i\in S} e_{ij}=c_j(S),
\]
provided the chain is chosen from the code order; equivalently, one may realize any monotone map from subsets to \(\{1,\dots,N\}\) as minima of \(N\) numbers.

A simpler explicit choice is to identify the \(N\) indices with \(1,\dots,N\), and for each \(j\) choose numbers \(e_{1j},\dots,e_{Nj}\) so that the map
\[
S\mapsto \min_{i\in S} e_{ij}
\]
gives any desired chain-valued monotone coordinate. Since every coordinate needs only \(N\) values, this is always possible.

Then define
\[
a_i=\prod_{j=1}^k p_j^{e_{ij}}.
\]
For every nonempty subset \(S\),
\[
g(S)=\prod_{j=1}^k p_j^{c_j(S)}.
\]
Since the codes \(c(S)\) are distinct, all these gcds are distinct.

Thus \(k\) primes suffice.

---

So the minimum possible number of distinct prime divisors is exactly
\[
\boxed{\left\lceil \log_N(2^N-1)\right\rceil }.
\]

Final answer: \(\left\lceil \log_N(2^N-1)\right\rceil\)
